what_it_does: This file is the MCP-facing tool wrapper for the Company noticeboard (cc_board). It registers a single multiplexed MCP tool that routes ten operations (file, list, get, transition, types, edit, comment, reply, thread, document) to the underlying runtime.cc_board module, validates required arguments, and returns structured dictionaries.
observations: MCP tool registered via @mcp.tool() inside a register(mcp, suite) hook intended for pkgutil auto-discovery.
All real logic is delegated to an imported runtime.cc_board module (aliased as cb).
Single function cc_board accepts a Literal op and a large flat set of optional parameters.
Supports threaded comments/replies and document assembly in addition to item lifecycle ops.
Uses board://<id> addresses for items and comment targets.
OPS tuple enumerates the supported operations.
standouts: Op-multiplexed single MCP tool covering filing, lifecycle transitions, threaded annotations, and document assembly.
Registry-driven validation is delegated to runtime; this file only checks presence of required arguments.
Comment/reply/thread ops treat board addresses as first-class annotation targets.
conventions: Follows the project pattern of a thin mcp_face tool that wraps a runtime implementation.
Uses Literal[...] for the op parameter and default empty strings for optional identifiers.
Imports the runtime module inside the tool function.
Docstring enumerates each op and its required/optional fields.
Returns structured dicts with op name and result data.
concerns: Large flat parameter list makes the signature broad and somewhat unwieldy.
The parameter name `type` shadows the built-in `type`.
Only cb.BoardError is caught; ValueError raised for missing/unknown ops propagates to the caller.
No local validation of `links`/`add_links` structure beyond passing them through.
notes: A thin MCP adapter that exposes the Company board's lifecycle, comments, and documents through one op-multiplexed tool.
questions: How does runtime.cc_board discover and load the item_types/, source_types/, and board_edges/ registries?
What is the exact format and generation rule for board://<id> addresses?
How are container `order` child addresses resolved and rendered?
Does runtime enforce link kind/target validation, or is it left to callers?
What is the relationship between board items and channel threads?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it is an agent-facing, op-multiplexed board tool that delegates to runtime policy.
apparent_themes: Separation of MCP surface from runtime engine
intention_signals: It tries to give agents a single, discoverable tool for filing and evolving meta-level work about the Company/MCP/CLI/CI/app, while keeping all policy, registries, and storage in a separate runtime layer.
novelty: The board is modeled as its own op-multiplexed MCP tool (like cc_clone beside cc_channel) with first-class comment/reply threading and document assembly, using board:// addresses and registry-referenced types/states.
contribution: It provides a stable MCP tool contract, parameter schema, and op router for board operations, plus required-field checks and error surfacing, that a fused system would keep as the agent-facing entry point.
summary_for_embedding: mcp_face/tools/cc_board.py registers a multiplexed MCP tool cc_board that routes filing, listing, lifecycle transitions, registries, edits, comments, replies, threads, and document assembly to runtime.cc_board.
intention_for_embedding: To expose the Company noticeboard as a single agent-facing MCP tool while delegating all policy and storage to the runtime board module.

symbols.does: Registers the cc_board MCP tool with the suite.
symbols.description: Outer registration hook expected by pkgutil auto-discovery; it decorates the inner cc_board function with @mcp.tool() and establishes the tool's parameter contract and operation routing.

symbols.does: Multiplexed MCP tool for filing, listing, transitioning, commenting on, and assembling board items.
symbols.description: Accepts an op and a wide set of optional fields, validates required arguments for each op, delegates all operations to runtime.cc_board, and returns structured dictionaries; catches runtime BoardError and surfaces it as an ok:false response.
